**Mento** is a style of [Jamaican](/wiki/Music_of_Jamaica "Music of Jamaica") [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") that predates and has greatly influenced [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") and [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") music.[[2]](#cite_note-2) It is a fusion of African rhythmic elements and European elements, which reached peak popularity in the 1940s and 1950s.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Mento typically features acoustic instruments, such as [acoustic guitar](/wiki/Classical_guitar "Classical guitar"), [banjo](/wiki/Banjo "Banjo"), [hand drums](/wiki/Hand_drum "Hand drum"), and the [rhumba box](/wiki/Rhumba_box "Rhumba box")—a large [mbira](/wiki/Mbira "Mbira") in the shape of a box that can be sat on while played. The rhumba box carries the [bass](/wiki/Bass_(sound) "Bass (sound)") part of the music.

Mento is often confused with [calypso](/wiki/Calypso_music "Calypso music"), a musical form from [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago"). Although the two share many similarities, they are separate and distinct musical forms. During the mid-20th century, mento was conflated with calypso, and mento was frequently [referred](/wiki/Metonym "Metonym") to as *calypso*, *kalypso* and *mento calypso*.[[4]](#cite_note-Shaping-4) Mento singers frequently used calypso songs and techniques. As in calypso, mento uses topical lyrics with a humorous slant, commenting on poverty and other social issues.[[4]](#cite_note-Shaping-4) Sexual innuendo is also common.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mento&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Mento draws on musical traditions brought by enslaved [West Africa](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa") people.[[4]](#cite_note-Shaping-4) Enslaved musicians were often required to play music for their masters and often rewarded for such skills.[[4]](#cite_note-Shaping-4) The Africans created a creole music, incorporating such elements of these traditions, including [quadrille](/wiki/Quadrille "Quadrille"), into their own [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music").[[4]](#cite_note-Shaping-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Gleaner1407-5)

The Jamaican mento style has a long history of conflation with Trinidadian [calypso](/wiki/Calypso_music "Calypso music"). The lyrics of mento songs often deal with aspects of everyday life in a light-hearted and humorous way. Many comment on poverty, poor housing, and other social issues. Thinly veiled sexual references and [innuendo](/wiki/Innuendo "Innuendo") are also common. Mento can be seen as a precursor of some of the movement motifs and themes dealing with such social issues found in modern [dancehall](/wiki/Dancehall "Dancehall"). It became more popular in the late 1940s, with mento performances becoming a common aspect of dances, parties and other events in Jamaica.[[5]](#cite_note-Gleaner1407-5)

The word *mento* is of uncertain [etymology](/wiki/Etymology "Etymology"); it may be from an [African language](/wiki/Languages_of_Africa "Languages of Africa") or [Cuban Spanish](/wiki/Cuban_Spanish "Cuban Spanish"); [Rex Nettleford](/wiki/Rex_Nettleford "Rex Nettleford") said the term was brought back from [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba") by Jamaicans returning from work there.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Supposedly, it derives from the Spanish verb *mentar*, "to mention, call out, name", because of the subtle ways that lyrics criticised people (whether fellow blacks, or the whites who were in charge).[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Major 1950s mento recording artists include [Louise Bennett](/wiki/Louise_Bennett "Louise Bennett"), [Count Lasher](/wiki/Count_Lasher "Count Lasher"), [Count Owen](/w/index.php?title=Count_Owen&action=edit&redlink=1 "Count Owen (page does not exist)"), [Harold Richardson](/w/index.php?title=Harold_Richardson_(singer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Harold Richardson (singer) (page does not exist)"), [Lord Flea](/wiki/Lord_Flea "Lord Flea"), [Lord Fly](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Fly&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lord Fly (page does not exist)"), [Alerth Bedasse](/w/index.php?title=Alerth_Bedasse&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alerth Bedasse (page does not exist)") with [Chin's Calypso Sextet](/w/index.php?title=Chin%27s_Calypso_Sextet&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chin's Calypso Sextet (page does not exist)"), [Laurel Aitken](/wiki/Laurel_Aitken "Laurel Aitken"), [Denzil Laing](/w/index.php?title=Denzil_Laing&action=edit&redlink=1 "Denzil Laing (page does not exist)"), [Lord Composer](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Composer&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lord Composer (page does not exist)"), [Lord Lebby](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Lebby&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lord Lebby (page does not exist)"), [Lord Power](/w/index.php?title=Lord_Power&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lord Power (page does not exist)"), [Hubert Porter](/w/index.php?title=Hubert_Porter&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hubert Porter (page does not exist)"), and [Harry Belafonte](/wiki/Harry_Belafonte "Harry Belafonte"), a New Yorker of Jamaican origin. His wildly popular hit records in 1956–1958, including "[Day-O (The Banana Boat Song)](/wiki/Day-O_(The_Banana_Boat_Song) "Day-O (The Banana Boat Song)")" and "[Jamaica Farewell](/wiki/Jamaica_Farewell "Jamaica Farewell")", were mento songs sold as calypso. Previously recorded Jamaican versions of many Belafonte's classic "calypso" hits can be heard on the *Jamaica – Mento 1951–1958* CD released by Frémeaux & Associés in 2009.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Due in part to Belafonte's popularity, mento became widely conflated with calypso in the 1950s. In a 1957 interview for *Calypso Star* magazine, [Lord Flea](/wiki/Lord_Flea "Lord Flea") said:

> In Jamaica, we call our music 'mento' until very recently. Today, 'calypso' is beginning to be used for [all kinds of West Indian music](/wiki/Metonym "Metonym"). This is because it's become so commercialized there. Some people like to think of West Indians as carefree natives who work and sing and play and laugh their lives away. But this isn't so. Most of the people there are hard working folks, and many of them are smart business men. If the tourists want 'calypso', that's what we sell them.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

This was the golden age of mento, as records pressed by [Stanley Motta](/wiki/Stanley_Motta "Stanley Motta"), [Ivan Chin](/w/index.php?title=Ivan_Chin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ivan Chin (page does not exist)"), [Ken Khouri](/wiki/Ken_Khouri "Ken Khouri") and others brought the music to a new audience. In the 1960s it became overshadowed by [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") and reggae. Mento is still played in Jamaica, especially in areas frequented by tourists. [Lloyd Bradley](/wiki/Lloyd_Bradley "Lloyd Bradley"), reggae historian and author of the seminal reggae book, *Bass Culture*, said that [Lee "Scratch" Perry](/wiki/Lee_%22Scratch%22_Perry "Lee \"Scratch\" Perry")'s seminal 1976 [dub](/wiki/Dub_music "Dub music") album, *[Super Ape](/wiki/Super_Ape "Super Ape")*, contained some of the purest mento influences he knew.[[11]](#cite_note-11) This style of music was revived in popularity by the [Jolly Boys](/wiki/Jolly_Boys "Jolly Boys") in the late 1980s and early 1990s with the release of four recordings on [First Warning Records](/w/index.php?title=First_Warning_Records&action=edit&redlink=1 "First Warning Records (page does not exist)")/[Rykodisc](/wiki/Rykodisc "Rykodisc") and a tour that included the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[5]](#cite_note-Gleaner1407-5) [Stanley Beckford](/wiki/Stanley_Beckford "Stanley Beckford") and Gilzene and the Blue Light Mento Band also revived rural mento in the 2000s. The mento dance is a Jamaican folk-form dance with acoustic guitar, banjo, hand drums and rhumba box.
